subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, be the circumcenter of a triangle with vertices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>6</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> denote the circumradius, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> denote the area and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> denote the perimeter of the triangle. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>γ</mi></math> is
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, be the circumcenter of a triangle with vertices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>6</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> denote the circumradius, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> denote the area and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> denote the perimeter of the triangle. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>γ</mi></math> is
solution:
Given: Aa,-2, Ba,6 and Ca4,-2

⇒AB2=a-a2+6+22

⇒AB2=64   ...i

⇒BC2=a-a42+6+22

⇒BC2=9a216+64   ...ii

⇒AC2=a-a42+-2+22

⇒AC2=9a216   ...iii

Using i, ii and iii,

⇒AC2+AB2=BC2

So, △ABC is right angled at A.

So, circumcentre will be mid-point of BC.

⇒S≡a+a42,6-22

⇒S≡5a8,2=5,a4

⇒a=8

⇒BC=9×6416+64

⇒BC=100=10

⇒AB=8

⇒AC=9×6416

⇒AC=6

Now, circumradius is given by,

R=BC2=5⇒α=5

Now, area of triangle will be β=12×6×8=24 and perimeter will be γ=6+8+10=24

⇒α=5, β=24, γ=24

⇒α+β+γ=53

subject: Mathematics
q: If the $x$-intercept of some line $L$ is double as that of the line, $3 x+4 y=12$ and the $y$-intercept of $L$ is half as that of the same line, then the slope of $L$ is :
question: If the $x$-intercept of some line $L$ is double as that of the line, $3 x+4 y=12$ and the $y$-intercept of $L$ is half as that of the same line, then the slope of $L$ is :
solution:
Given line $3 x+4 y=12$ can be rewritten as
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{3 x}{12}+\frac{4 y}{12}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{x}{4}+\frac{y}{3}=1 \\
& \Rightarrow x \text {-intercept }=4 \text { and } y \text {-intercept }=3
\end{aligned}
$$
Let the required line be
$\mathrm{L}: \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$ where
$a=x$-intercept and $b=y$-intercept
According to the question
$a=4 \times 2=8$ and $b=3 / 2$
$\therefore$ Required line is $\frac{x}{8}+\frac{2 y}{3}=1$
$\Rightarrow 3 x+16 y=24$
$\Rightarrow y=\frac{-3}{16} x+\frac{24}{16}$
Hence, required slope $=\frac{-3}{16}$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the points of intersections of the lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>11</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> are the mid points of the sides of a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ABC</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ABC</mi></math> is
question: Let the points of intersections of the lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>11</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> are the mid points of the sides of a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ABC</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ABC</mi></math> is
solution:
If P, Q, R are mid-points of sides of ∆ABC then  4×Area(ΔPQR)=Area(∆ABC)

Area of  ΔABC=4×12a1 b1c1a2 b2c2a3 b3c32C1C2C3 

Where C1,C2,C3 are cofactors of c1,c2,c3

 ΔABC=4×121-111-232-5112(-1)(3)(-1)

=2×9÷3

=6

question: If $\mathrm{P}(6,1)$ be the orthocentre of the triangle whose vertices are $\mathrm{A}(5,-2), \mathrm{B}(8,3)$ and $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$, then the point $C$ lies on the circle:
solution: Slope of $A D=3$ Slope of $\mathrm{BC}=-\frac{1}{3}$ equation of $\mathrm{BC}=3 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{x}-17=0$ slope of $\mathrm{BE}=1$ Slope of $\mathrm{AC}=-1$ equation of $\mathrm{AC}$ is $\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}-3=0$ point $C$ is $(-4,7)$

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the triangles with vertices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. If the maximum and the minimum perimeters of such triangles are obtained at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>β</mi></math> respectively, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>21</mn><mi>β</mi></math> is equal to ___________.
question: Consider the triangles with vertices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. If the maximum and the minimum perimeters of such triangles are obtained at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>β</mi></math> respectively, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>21</mn><mi>β</mi></math> is equal to ___________.
solution:
Given,

The triangles with vertices A2, 1, B0, 0 and Ct, 4, t=0, 4,

And the maximum and the minimum perimeters of such triangles are obtained at t=α and t=β respectively,

Now to minimise CA+CB, in below diagram take image of B in y=4,

We get, B'=0, 8

 

Now finding, equation of AB' we get,

y-8=-72x-0

Now, putting y=4 in above equation we get,

-4=-72x

⇒x=87⇒β=87

Now, maximum perimeter will be possible if α=0 or 4

Now taking α=0 we get,

AB=5, BC=4 & AC=13

Now when α=4 we get,

AB=5, BC=42 & AC=13

Now on comparing the perimeter we get, maximum perimeter at α=4

Hence, 6α+21β=48

q: Two sides of a parallelogram are along the lines, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If its diagonals intersect at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then one of its vertex is:
question: Two sides of a parallelogram are along the lines, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If its diagonals intersect at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then one of its vertex is:
solution:
As BC∥AD⇒SlopeBC=SlopeAD

Hence, slope of BC=1⇒3-h-5h-4=1

⇒-h-2=h-4⇒ 2h=2⇒h=1

∴ B1,2 and D3,6

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> be a diameter of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are the lengths of the perpendiculars from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> on the straight line, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> respectively, then the maximum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></math> is _______
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> be a diameter of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are the lengths of the perpendiculars from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> on the straight line, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> respectively, then the maximum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></math> is _______
solution:
Let P(3cosθ,3sinθ)∴Q(-3cosθ,-3sinθ)
given line x+y-2=0 ∴α=|3cosθ+3sinθ-2|2
β=|-3cosθ-3sinθ-2|2
∴  αβ=(3cosθ+3sinθ-2)·(3cosθ+3sinθ+2)2=9(1+sin2θ)-42
∴ maximum of αβ=7

q: Let <math><mi>A</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>B</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mi>C</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> be the vertices of a triangle <math><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><mi>P</mi></math> is a point inside the triangle <math><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> such that the triangles <math><mi>A</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>A</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi><mi></mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>C</mi></math> have equal areas, then the length of the line segment <math><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> where <math><mi>Q</mi></math> is the point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> is
question: Let <math><mi>A</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>B</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mi>C</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> be the vertices of a triangle <math><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><mi>P</mi></math> is a point inside the triangle <math><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> such that the triangles <math><mi>A</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>A</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi><mi></mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>C</mi></math> have equal areas, then the length of the line segment <math><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> where <math><mi>Q</mi></math> is the point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> is
solution:
P must be centroid of ∆ABC
∴P176,83⇒PQ=2462+93=5 units

subject: Mathematics
q: A straight line through the point A(3, 4) is such that its intercept between the axes is bisected at A. Its equation is
question: A straight line through the point A(3, 4) is such that its intercept between the axes is bisected at A. Its equation is
solution:
The equation of axes is $x y=0$
$\Rightarrow$ the equation of the line is $\frac{x \cdot 4+y \cdot 3}{2}=12 \Rightarrow 4 x+3 y=24$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the equation of the locus of a point equidistant from the point $\left(a_1, b_1\right)$ and $\left(a_2, b_2\right)$ is $\left(a_1-b_2\right) x+\left(a_1-b_2\right) y+c=0$, then the value of 'c' is
question: If the equation of the locus of a point equidistant from the point $\left(a_1, b_1\right)$ and $\left(a_2, b_2\right)$ is $\left(a_1-b_2\right) x+\left(a_1-b_2\right) y+c=0$, then the value of 'c' is
solution:
$\left(h-a_1\right)^2+\left(k-b_1\right)^2=\left(h-a_2\right)^2+\left(k-b_2\right)^2$
$\left(a_1-a_2\right) x+\left(b_1-b_2\right) y+\frac{1}{2}\left(a_2^2+b_2^2-a_1^2-b_1^2\right)=0$
$C=\frac{1}{2}\left(a_2^2+b_2^2-a_1^2-b_1^2\right)$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $A(3,1,-1), B\left(\frac{5}{3}, \frac{7}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right), C(2,2,1)$ and $D\left(\frac{10}{3}, \frac{2}{3}, \frac{-1}{3}\right)$ are the vertices of a quadrilateral $A B C D$, then its area is
question: If $A(3,1,-1), B\left(\frac{5}{3}, \frac{7}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right), C(2,2,1)$ and $D\left(\frac{10}{3}, \frac{2}{3}, \frac{-1}{3}\right)$ are the vertices of a quadrilateral $A B C D$, then its area is
solution: Area $=\frac{1}{2}|\overline{\mathrm{BD}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}|$ $\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{BD}}=\frac{5}{3} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{5}{3} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\frac{2}{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the straight lines $x+3 y=4,3 x+y=4$ and $x+y$ $=0$ form a triangle, then the triangle is
question: If the straight lines $x+3 y=4,3 x+y=4$ and $x+y$ $=0$ form a triangle, then the triangle is
solution:
Let equation of $A B: x+3 y=4$
Let equation of $B C: 3 x+y=4$
Let equation of $C A: x+y=0$
Now, By solving these equations we get $A=(-2,2), B=(1,1)$ and $C=(2,-2)$
Now, $A B=\sqrt{9+1}=\sqrt{10}$,
$$
B C=\sqrt{1+9}=\sqrt{10}
$$
and $C A=\sqrt{16+16}=\sqrt{32}$
Since, length of $A B$ and $B C$ are same
therefore triangle is isosceles.

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> be given three points. question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> be given three points. A line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> , intersects lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> respectively. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the areas of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>C</mi></math> respectively, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is equal to :
solution:
P≡x1,mx1

 Q≡x2,mx2

A1=12341201-111=132

A2=12x1mx11x2mx21201

A2=122mx1-mx2=mx1-x2

A1=3 A2⇒132=3 mx1-x2

⇒  x1-x2=166 m

AC: x+3 y=2

BC: y=4 x-8

P:x+3y=2 & y=mx⇒x1=21+3m

Q: y=4x-8 & y=mx⇒x2=84-m

x1-x2=21+3m-84-m

=-26 m1+3m4-m=26 m3m+1m-4

=26 m3m+14-m

x1-x2=136 m

26 m3m+14-m=136 m

⇒  12m2=-3m+1m-4

⇒  12m2=-3m2-11m-4

⇒  15m2-11m-4=0

⇒  15m2-15m+4m-4=0

⇒  15m+4m-1=0

⇒  m=1

solution:

Equation of angle bisector will be, 3x-4y+1232+42=8x+6y+1182+62

q: Let $\mathrm{A}(1,2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(-3,-6)$ be two diagonally opposite vertices of a rhombus, whose sides AD and BC are parallel to the line $7 x-y=14$. If $\mathrm{B}(\alpha, \beta)$ and $\mathrm{D}(\gamma, \delta)$ are the other two vertices, then $|\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta|$ is equal to
question: Let $\mathrm{A}(1,2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(-3,-6)$ be two diagonally opposite vertices of a rhombus, whose sides AD and BC are parallel to the line $7 x-y=14$. If $\mathrm{B}(\alpha, \beta)$ and $\mathrm{D}(\gamma, \delta)$ are the other two vertices, then $|\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta|$ is equal to
solution:
Midpoint of diagonal $AC$ is $M = (-1,-2)$. Slope of $AC = \frac{-6-2}{-3-1} = 2$.
In a rhombus, diagonals are perpendicular bisectors. So $BD$ has slope $-\frac{1}{2}$ and passes through $M$: $y + 2 = -\frac{1}{2}(x+1)$.
$AD$ has slope 7: $\frac{\delta - 2}{\gamma - 1} = 7 \Rightarrow \delta = 7\gamma - 5$.
$D$ lies on $BD$: $\delta = -\frac{\gamma}{2} - \frac{5}{2}$.
Solving: $7\gamma - 5 = -\frac{\gamma}{2} - \frac{5}{2} \Rightarrow \gamma = \frac{1}{3}$, $\delta = -\frac{8}{3}$.
$B = (2(-1)-\frac{1}{3},\, 2(-2)+\frac{8}{3}) = (-\frac{7}{3}, -\frac{4}{3})$.
$|\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta| = \left|-\frac{7}{3}-\frac{4}{3}+\frac{1}{3}-\frac{8}{3}\right| = |-6| = 6$.

q: If the three lines $x-3 y=p, a x+2 y=q$ and $a x+y=r$ form a right-angled triangle then :
question: If the three lines $x-3 y=p, a x+2 y=q$ and $a x+y=r$ form a right-angled triangle then :
solution:
Since three lines $x-3 y=p$, $a x+2 y=q$ and $a x+y=r$ form a right angled triangle $\therefore$ product of slopes of any two lines $=-1$ Suppose $a x+2 y=q$ and $x-3 y=p$ are $\perp$ to each other.
$$
\therefore \frac{-a}{2} \times \frac{1}{3}=-1 \Rightarrow a=6
$$
Now, consider option one by one $a=6$ satisfies only option (a)
$\therefore$ Required answer is $a^2-9 a+18=0$

q: Let the equations of two adjacent sides of a parallelogram <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>23</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>23</mn></math>. If the equation of its one diagonal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>23</mn></math>  and the distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> from the other diagonal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><msup><mi>d</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to ______________
question: Let the equations of two adjacent sides of a parallelogram <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>23</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>23</mn></math>. If the equation of its one diagonal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>23</mn></math>  and the distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> from the other diagonal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><msup><mi>d</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to ______________
solution:
Given,

The equations of two adjacent sides of a parallelogram ABCD be 2x-3y=-23 and 5x+4y=23,

So, AB≡2x-3y=-23 and BC≡5x+4y=23

Also given, AC≡3x+7y=23

Solving the above lines we get, A(–4, 5), B(–1, 7), C(3, 2)

We know that,

Diagonal of parallelogram have same midpoint,

So AC and BD have same mid-point and let point D be x,y,

So midpoint formula we get, 

x-12=-4+32⇒x=0 and y+72=2+52⇒y=0

Hence, point D is (0, 0)

Now Equation of BD will be 7x+y=0

Now finding the distance of A-4,5 from 7x+y=0 we get,

d=7(-4)+572+12=2350

Hence, 50d2=232=529

If the focus of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi></math> passing through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are rational numbers, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

A(0,1), B(1,1) and C(1,0) be the mid-points of the sides of a triangle with incentre at the point D,

Now finding the vertices of the triangle by using midpoint formula and plotting the diagram we get,

Now finding the incentre of the above triangle using the formula,

I≡ax1+bx2+cx3a+b+c,ay1+by2+cy3a+b+c

We get, Incentre D=44+22,44+22

Now given parabola y2=4ax passes through the incentre D we get,

44+222=4a44+22

⇒a=14+22

Now we know that focus of parabola is given by, focus (a, 0)

Hence, focus will be,14+22,0=4-228,0


Now comparing with (α+β2,0) we get,

α=48=12, β=-14
Hence, αβ2=12-142=162=8

If<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>B</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>90</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mo>,</mo></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>ar</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>Δ</mi><mi>ABC</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> sq. units, then the abscissa of the vertex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is :
solution:
mAC=β−2α−1

mAB=2−11−3=−12

AB⊥AC

∴ β−2α−1−12=−1

β=2α−2+2

β=2α

Now area of ΔABC=55=12AB.AC

⇒ 123−12+1−22.α−12+β−22=55

⇒ α−12+2α−22=10

⇒ α−125=10

⇒ α−1=25

⇒ α=1±25.

subject: Mathematics
q: In a triangle, the sum of lengths of two sides is $\mathrm{x}$ and the product of the lengths of the same two sides is $y$. if $x^{2}-c^{2}=y,$ where $c$ is the length of the third side of the triangle, then the circumradius of the triangle is
question: In a triangle, the sum of lengths of two sides is $\mathrm{x}$ and the product of the lengths of the same two sides is $y$. if $x^{2}-c^{2}=y,$ where $c$ is the length of the third side of the triangle, then the circumradius of the triangle is
solution:
Let two sides of triangle are $a$ and $b$.

$$

\begin{aligned}

& a+b=x \\

& a b=y \\

& x^{2}-c^{2}=y \Rightarrow(a+b)^{2}-c^{2}=a b \\

\Rightarrow &(a+b-c)(a+b+c)=a b \\

\Rightarrow & 2(s-c)(2 s)=a b \\

\Rightarrow & 4 s(s-c)=a b \\

\Rightarrow & \frac{s(s-c)}{a b}=\frac{1}{4} \\

\Rightarrow & \cos ^{2} \frac{c}{2}=\frac{1}{4} \\

\Rightarrow & \cos c=-\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow c=120^{\circ}

\end{aligned}

$$

$\therefore$ Area of triangle is,

$\Delta=\frac{1}{2} a b\left(\sin 120^{\circ}\right)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a b$

$\because \quad R=\frac{a b c}{4 \Delta}$

$\because \quad \mathrm{R}=\frac{a b c}{\sqrt{3} a b}=\frac{c}{\sqrt{3}}$

Now let Px,y be a point on BC, so area formed by △ABP will be,

 Δ1=12xy1111-431=12-2x-5y+7

And area of triangle formed by △ABC will be, Δ2=12111-431-2-51=362

Given Δ1Δ2=47⇒-2x-5y+736=47

⇒  14x+35y=-95     ⋯1

Equation of BC by two point form will be 4x+y=-13      ⋯2

Solving equation 1 and 2 we get,

Point P-207,-117

Now equation of AP will be y-1=1+1171+207x-1

⇒3y-2x=1,

So x-intercept will be point Q-12,0 and similarly from AC we get R12,0

So Area of triangle AQR=12×1×1=12

subject: Mathematics
q: Let two straight lines drawn from the origin $\mathrm{O}$ intersect the line $3 x+4 y=12$ at the points $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{Q}$ such that $\triangle \mathrm{OPQ}$ is an isosceles triangle and $\angle \mathrm{POQ}=90^{\circ}$. If $l=\mathrm{OP}^2+\mathrm{PQ}^2+\mathrm{QO}^2$, then the greatest integer less than or equal to $l$ is :
question: Let two straight lines drawn from the origin $\mathrm{O}$ intersect the line $3 x+4 y=12$ at the points $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{Q}$ such that $\triangle \mathrm{OPQ}$ is an isosceles triangle and $\angle \mathrm{POQ}=90^{\circ}$. If $l=\mathrm{OP}^2+\mathrm{PQ}^2+\mathrm{QO}^2$, then the greatest integer less than or equal to $l$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 3 \mathrm{x}+4 \mathrm{y}=12 \\ & 3(\mathrm{r} \cos \theta)+4(\mathrm{r} \sin \theta)=12 \\ & \mathrm{r}(3 \cos \theta+4 \sin \theta)=12 \ldots(1) \\ & 3(-\mathrm{r} \sin \theta)+4(\mathrm{r} \cos \theta)=12 \\ & \mathrm{r}(-3 \sin \theta+4 \cos \theta)=12 \ldots(2) \\ & \left(\frac{12}{\mathrm{r}}\right)^2+\left(\frac{12}{\mathrm{r}}\right)^2=(3 \cos \theta+4 \sin \theta)^2+(-3 \sin \theta+4 \cos \theta)^2 \\ & 2\left(\frac{12}{\mathrm{r}}\right)^2=9+16 \\ & \frac{2 \times 144}{\mathrm{r}^2}=25 \Rightarrow 288=25 \mathrm{r}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{288}{25}=\mathrm{r}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{2}\left(\frac{12}{5}\right)=\mathrm{r} \\ & \ell=\mathrm{OP}^2+\mathrm{PQ}^2+\mathrm{QO}^2 \\ & \ell=\mathrm{r}^2+\mathrm{r}^2 \quad+\mathrm{r}^2(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)^2+\mathrm{r}^2(\sin \theta+\cos \theta)^2 \\ & =2 \mathrm{r}^2+\mathrm{r}^2(1+\sin 2 \theta+1-2 \sin 2 \theta) \\ & =2 \mathrm{r}^2+2 \mathrm{r}^2 \\ & =4 \mathrm{r}^2 \\ & =4\left(\frac{288}{25}\right)=\frac{1152}{25}=46.08 \\ & {[\ell]=46}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Among the statements
$(S 1)$ : If $A(5,-1)$ and $B(-2,3)$ are two vertices of a triangle, whose orthocentre is $(0,0)$, then its third vertex is $(-4,-7)$
and
(S2) : If positive numbers 2a, b, c are three consecutive terms of an A.P., then the lines $\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{by}+\mathrm{c}=0$ are concurrent at $(2,-2)$,
question:
Among the statements
$(S 1)$ : If $A(5,-1)$ and $B(-2,3)$ are two vertices of a triangle, whose orthocentre is $(0,0)$, then its third vertex is $(-4,-7)$
and
(S2) : If positive numbers 2a, b, c are three consecutive terms of an A.P., then the lines $\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{by}+\mathrm{c}=0$ are concurrent at $(2,-2)$,
solution:
Statement 1
$m_{AO} \cdot m_{BC} = -1$
$B(-2, 3)$
$A(5, -1) \qquad C(h, k)$

$2a, b, c \rightarrow$ A.P.
$b = \dfrac{2a + c}{2}$
$\Rightarrow 2a - 2b + c = 0$
$\therefore$ lines $ax + by + c = 0$ are concurrent then
$\dfrac{x}{2} = \dfrac{y}{-2} = \dfrac{1}{1}$
$x = 2$ and $y = -2$
$\therefore$ Point of concurrency is $(2, -2)$
$\therefore$ Statement 2 is correct.

subject: Mathematics
q: The set of all possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> for which the points<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> lie on the same side of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is?
question: The set of all possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> for which the points<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> lie on the same side of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is?
solution:
We know that if Ax1,y1 & Bx2,y2 lies on the same side to line L=0, then LAx1,y1 LBx2,y2>0.

Given, the points A1,2 & Bsinθ,cosθ lies on the same side of the line x+y-1=0.

∴1+2-1sinθ+cosθ-1>0

⇒sinθ+cosθ>1

⇒sinθ×12+cosθ×12>1×12

⇒sinθ.cosπ4+cosθ.sinπ4>12

⇒sinθ+π4>12

⇒π4<θ+π4<3π4

⇒0<θ<π2

⇒θ∈0,π2

subject: Mathematics
q: The perpendicular bisector of the line segment joining $P(1,4)$ and $Q(k, 3)$ has $y$-intercept - 4. Then a possible value of $k$ is
question: The perpendicular bisector of the line segment joining $P(1,4)$ and $Q(k, 3)$ has $y$-intercept - 4. Then a possible value of $k$ is
solution:
Slope of bisector $=k-1$
Middle point $=\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}+1}{2}, \frac{7}{2}\right)$
Equation of bisector is
$y-\frac{7}{2}=(k-1)\left(x-\frac{(k+1)}{2}\right)$
Put $\mathrm{x}=0$ and $\mathrm{y}=-4$
$$
\Rightarrow k=\pm 4
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> be such that its distance from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is thrice the distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> If the locus of the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> (in the nearest integer) is equal to __________.
question: Let a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> be such that its distance from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is thrice the distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> If the locus of the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> (in the nearest integer) is equal to __________.
solution:
Let point is h,k

So, h-52+k2=3h+52+k2

8x2+8y2+100x+200=0

x2+y2+252x+25=0

r2=25242-25

4r2=2524-100

4r2=156.25-100

4r2=56.25

After round off 4r2=56

q: A line makes the same angle $\theta$, with each of the $x$ and $z$ axis. If the angle $\beta$, which it makes with $y$-axis, is such that $\sin ^2 \beta=3 \sin ^2 \theta$, then $\cos ^2 \theta$ equals
question: A line makes the same angle $\theta$, with each of the $x$ and $z$ axis. If the angle $\beta$, which it makes with $y$-axis, is such that $\sin ^2 \beta=3 \sin ^2 \theta$, then $\cos ^2 \theta$ equals
solution:
$I=\cos \theta, m=\cos \theta, n=\cos \beta$
$\cos ^2 \theta+\cos ^2 \theta+\cos ^2 \beta=1 \Rightarrow 2 \cos ^2 \theta=\sin ^2 \beta=3 \sin ^2 \theta \quad$ (given)
$\cos ^2 \theta=3 / 5$
